articles.title: How to protect yourself from heat-related illnesses - IslanderNews.com
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
articles.full_text:
Hydration, shade and rest are three important actions to take as the extreme heat of Summer settles into our daily lives.
It is very hot outside and the humidity makes it feel hotter. This extreme heat, and the body’s inability to cool down, can lead to mild heat-related illnesses like heat rash, heat cramps or more serious illnesses like heat exhaustion and heat stroke, the latter of which can be fatal.
Extreme heat can affect anyone, but some risk factors can make a person more susceptible to such as being an outside worker or athlete. Also, extreme age (infants, young children, persons older than 65), inadequate acclimatization, pregnancy, low income, persons with medical conditions (cardiovascular disease, diabetes, asthma, mental health disorders, physical disabilities) and some medications can make you more sensitive to heat.
Preventive actions you can take are Hydration, Shade and Rest.
• Stay hydrated with water or drinks with electrolytes, like sports drinks (if no contraindication due to a medical problem) and drink even if you aren’t thirsty.
• Limit drinks that can make you dehydrated: sugary drinks, caffeine and alcohol.
• Stay in air conditioning but if you are outside (preferably early morning), seek shade.
• Rest if you have been outside and feel hot.
• If you aren’t used to the heat and humidity, acclimate by gradually spending a few minutes and eventually hours outside each day.
• Wear light, loose clothing, a wide brimmed hat and sunscreen.
• Check with your health care provider to see if your medications make you more sensitive to heat illness.
• Use a ‘buddy system’ when outside, so you can watch each other for signs and symptoms of heat illness.
• Recognize heat illness symptoms: Know the difference between heat exhaustion and life-threatening heat stroke.
• Heat exhaustion signs: Dizziness, heavy sweating, nausea, cold/clammy skin, fast pulse.
• Heat stroke signs (Medical Emergency!): High body temperature (104°F+), hot/red/dry skin (no sweating), confusion, headache, fainting.
First Aid for Heat Illness
Move the person to shade or a cooler place, lay them down, cool their body with ice or cold water by pouring water on their head, wetting a cloth and putting it around their neck, on their head, under their arms, or groin area. Do not give them sips of water if you suspect heat stroke and call 911.
Carol Lindsey is a Key Biscayne resident, Family Nurse Practitioner and Member of the Florida Clinicians for Climate Action.
